# Ahmed Mohammed — AI/ML Engineer & Entrepreneur

> AI/ML Engineer and entrepreneur based in Linz, Austria. M.Sc. Artificial Intelligence from Johannes Kepler University Linz (supervised by Prof. Sepp Hochreiter, inventor of LSTM). Founder of Faultrix. Specializing in computer vision, diffusion models, out-of-distribution detection, and industrial defect detection.

## Identity

- **Full name:** Ahmed Mohammed
- **Location:** Linz, Upper Austria, Austria
- **Email:** ahmed.mo.0595@gmail.com
- **Portfolio:** https://ahmed-3m.github.io
- **Company:** Faultrix (Founder) — https://faultrix.com
- **Open to:** Senior AI/ML roles · Research collaborations · Enterprise partnerships

## Key Achievements

- 99.03% AUROC on CIFAR-10 OOD detection benchmark (Master's Thesis, JKU Linz, 2026)
- Introduced class-conditional separation loss improving OOD AUROC by +18.8 percentage points over baseline
- 98.4% defect detection accuracy in real-time industrial production environment (PROFACTOR GmbH, Zer0P project, Upper Austria government funded)
- Built Faultrix solo from zero to production — AI construction QC SaaS generating ÖNORM-compliant reports in under 1 minute

## Projects

- [Faultrix](https://faultrix.com): AI-powered construction quality control SaaS. Analyzes building photos, generates ÖNORM B 2110-compliant technical reports in under 1 minute. SHA-256 evidence chain, DSGVO compliant, AES-256 encryption. Stack: Next.js, Convex, OpenAI API, Cloudflare R2, Stripe, Clerk, Docker.
- [OOD Detection Framework](https://github.com/ahmed-3m/OOD-diffusion-detector): Novel use of conditional diffusion models as generative classifiers for out-of-distribution detection. 99.03% AUROC on CIFAR-10. Introduced class-conditional separation loss. Applied to industrial quality control with multi-head conditioning.
- [Industrial Anomaly Detection](https://ahmed-3m.github.io/Diffusion-Based%20Multi-class%20Defect%20Detection.pdf): YOLO + diffusion model pipeline for real-time industrial defect detection on inkjet-printed building components. 98.4% accuracy in production. Part of Zer0P project (PROFACTOR GmbH).
- [EEG Signal Classification](https://github.com/ahmed-3m/Motor-Imagery-classification): LSTM, Bi-LSTM, GRU architectures for EEG motor imagery classification in brain-computer interface applications. Hyperparameter optimization across architectures (Karunya University, 2023).

## Research & Publications

## Full Blog Post Content

### Blog: How I Hit 99.03% AUROC on OOD Detection Using Conditional Diffusion Models
URL: https://ahmed-3m.github.io/blog/ood-diffusion-thesis/
Date: 2026-03-20
Tags: Diffusion Models, OOD Detection, Deep Learning, PyTorch, CIFAR-10, Generative Models, JKU Linz

My Master's thesis at JKU Linz (under Prof. Sepp Hochreiter, inventor of LSTM) introduced class-conditional separation loss into conditional diffusion models used as generative classifiers — improving out-of-distribution detection from 80.25% to 99.03% AUROC on CIFAR-10, an 18.8 percentage-point gain.

Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is the ability of a neural network to say "I don't know" when it sees something it was never trained on. My key contribution was a class-conditional separation loss λ·L_sep that explicitly encourages the model's intermediate representations to cluster by class and push different classes apart.

Ablation results on CIFAR-10:
- λ=0.0 (baseline): 80.25% AUROC
- λ=0.001: 97.32%
- λ=0.01: 98.69%
- λ=0.02 (best): 99.11%
- λ=0.05: 98.51%
- λ=0.1: 96.67%

The reported thesis result of 99.03% AUROC is the average across 5 OOD test datasets (SVHN, LSUN, iSUN, Textures, Places365) against CIFAR-10 as in-distribution.

Applied to industrial data from PROFACTOR GmbH's Zer0P project: industrial baseline AUROC was 86.73% ± 2.3% (5-fold CV), improved to 87.3% ± 2.1% — a small, statistically non-significant improvement. This cross-domain analysis is a key thesis contribution.

Implementation: PyTorch + PyTorch Lightning, DDPM U-Net with class conditioning, Hydra + Weights & Biases, trained on JKU GPU cluster (RTX A6000).

Open-sourced at github.com/ahmed-3m/OOD-diffusion-detector. Full thesis PDF: ahmed-3m.github.io/Mohammed_Ahmed_Thesis_Diffusion_OOD_Detection.pdf

---

### Blog: Diffusion Models for Industrial Defect Detection: 98.4% Accuracy at PROFACTOR GmbH
URL: https://ahmed-3m.github.io/blog/diffusion-models-anomaly-detection/
Date: 2024-11-20
Tags: Diffusion Models, Anomaly Detection, YOLOv8, Industrial AI, Computer Vision, Quality Control

At PROFACTOR GmbH (Steyr, Austria), I built a machine vision pipeline that detects defects in inkjet-printed building components with 98.4% accuracy in a real-time production environment. Part of Zer0P — a zero-defect manufacturing initiative funded by the Government of Upper Austria.

Architecture: YOLOv8 as feature backbone (not detector) → feature maps fed into conditional diffusion model as generative classifier. Multi-head conditioning: one learned embedding per feature type (8 types: angle, dist1, dist6, dots, edge1-4), all sharing the same U-Net backbone.

Results by feature type: dots 0.963 AUROC, dist6 0.941, edge3 0.875 (+13.1% improvement). Overall AUROC: 86.7% baseline → 87.3% with separation loss. The 98.4% accuracy refers to binary classification at FPR=5% in production.

Production deployment: Docker container on NVIDIA Jetson AGX, ~35ms per component inference, 28 components/minute throughput, 6 weeks continuous uptime. FP16 precision (INT8 had occasional instability).

Key lessons: (1) Generative models beat discriminative ones when defect examples are scarce, (2) Feature type matters more than architecture, (3) Statistical rigor with 5-fold CV + Holm correction is non-negotiable, (4) Edge deployment is fundamentally different from server deployment.

Full technical report: ahmed-3m.github.io/Diffusion-Based%20Multi-class%20Defect%20Detection.pdf

Industrial dataset: https://zenodo.org/records/11444566 (FTI_Zer0P Dataset 2023, Profactor GmbH, CC-BY-4.0)

---

### Blog: YOLOv8 for Industrial Quality Control: Architecture Decisions That Moved the Needle
URL: https://ahmed-3m.github.io/blog/computer-vision-yolo-mastery/
Date: 2024-10-20
Tags: Computer Vision, YOLOv8, Deep Learning, Industrial AI, PyTorch, Production ML

The specific architecture choices, data engineering decisions, and production deployment tricks that contributed to 98.4% defect detection accuracy at PROFACTOR GmbH.

Key decisions:
1. YOLOv8 as feature extractor (not detector) — extracted bottleneck features after layer 9, before detection head
2. Data: 1,327 components across 8 feature types, 70-80% GOOD / 20-30% BAD. Effective augmentations: ±20% brightness/contrast, horizontal/vertical flips, CutMix between GOOD samples. Unsuccessful: synthetic defect generation (GANs), heavy geometric augmentation
3. Training: freeze backbone for 10 epochs, then unfreeze at 10x lower LR. lr=1e-4, batch_size=16, cosine scheduler, early stopping patience=15
4. Evaluation: 5-fold stratified CV (seed=42), AUROC as primary metric, per-feature breakdown more informative than single accuracy figure
5. Production: INT8 quantization via TensorRT achieved 18ms latency but showed instability; FP16 at 35ms used in production. Ring buffer for streaming inference. Alert threshold at FPR < 5%.

What I'd do differently: (1) Collect more BAD samples from day one, (2) Add Monte Carlo Dropout as confidence signal, (3) Log everything from the start with W&B.

---

### Blog: Shipping Faultrix: What I Learned Building an AI SaaS From Zero in 5 Months
URL: https://ahmed-3m.github.io/blog/5-month-llm-adventure/
Date: 2025-11-15
Tags: LLM, SaaS, Next.js, OpenAI, Production AI, Faultrix, Entrepreneurship

Faultrix is an AI-powered construction quality control SaaS. A building inspector uploads photos; Faultrix generates a legally compliant ÖNORM B 2110 technical report in under 1 minute with SHA-256 evidence chain, DSGVO compliance, AES-256 encryption.

Tech stack: Next.js 14 App Router + TypeScript, Convex (database/backend), Clerk (auth), Cloudflare R2 (file storage), OpenAI API GPT-4o + Vision (AI analysis), Stripe (payments).

The hardest part wasn't the AI — it was ÖNORM compliance (3 weeks iterating with real building inspectors). The SHA-256 evidence chain UX is also non-trivial.

What transfers from research: systematic experimentation, evaluation rigor, understanding model limitations. What doesn't transfer: product intuition, distribution shift as UX problem, can't ablate your way to product-market fit.

Key lesson: Ship the simplest version that delivers core value first. The first version of Faultrix had a beautiful AI pipeline but terrible UX requiring 8 upload steps. Users churned before reaching the AI.

Try it at faultrix.com. Feedback welcome: ahmed.mo.0595@gmail.com.
